# 418 I'm a teapot

The HTTP **`418 I'm a teapot`** status response code indicates that the server refuses to brew coffee because it is, permanently, a teapot. A combined coffee/tea pot that is temporarily out of coffee should instead return [`503`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Reference/Status/503). This error is a reference to Hyper Text Coffee Pot Control Protocol defined in April Fools' jokes in 1998 and 2014.

While originally defined in [RFC 2324](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc2324) as an April Fools' joke, this status code was formally reserved in [RFC 9110](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9110) due to its wide deployment as a joke, so it cannot be assigned any non-joke semantics for the foreseeable future.

Some websites use this response for requests they do not wish to handle, such as automated queries.

## In this article

*   [Status](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Status/418#status)
*   [Specifications](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Status/418#specifications)
*   [See also](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Status/418#see_also)

## [Status](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Status/418#status)

http

```
418 I'm a teapot
```

## [Specifications](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Status/418#specifications)

| Specification |
| --- |
| [RFC 2324 # section-2.3.2](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc2324#section-2.3.2) |
| [HTTP Semantics # name-418-unused](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9110#name-418-unused) |

Based on the information from MDN, here is the research on HTTP status code 418:

1.  **What the code means:** The HTTP `418 I'm a teapot` status code indicates that the server refuses to brew coffee because it is, permanently, a teapot.
2.  **When it was created:** It was originally defined in **1998** (specifically in RFC 2324) as part of an April Fools' joke.
3.  **Is it a joke or real status code?** It originated as an **April Fools' joke** (referencing the "Hyper Text Coffee Pot Control Protocol"). However, because it was so widely deployed as a joke across the internet, it was **formally reserved** in RFC 9110. This means it is a "real" reserved code in the sense that it cannot be assigned any other official, non-joke meaning for the foreseeable future.
